coredata.dc:title: Role of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as a green photosensitizer with Ag-doped ZnO nanorods for improved electrochemical solar energy conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Majumder, Tanmoy
Dhar, Saurab
Debnath, Kamalesh
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties of Ag doped ZnO nanorods (Ag-ZnO NRs) have been studied in details. Ag-ZnO NRs photoanodes demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency (i.e. photon to hydrogen), photoresponse properties, incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE), compared to pristine ZnO nanorods. S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal process and utilized as a green photosensitizer for further improvement of photoelectrochemical activities. S,N-GQD sensitized nanorods showed photoconversion efficiency (η) ∼1.1%, and short circuit photocurrent (JSC ) ∼0.96mA/cm2, which are 3.4 times and 2.7 times higher than ZnO NRs. Sensitization with S,N-GQDs also improved the IPCE value and attained maximum 62.8% at 350nm, which is 2.2 times higher compared to ZnO NRs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO nanorods
Ag doped ZnO nanorods
Photoelectrochemical cell
S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots
Photosensitizer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrographs of hydrothermally grown (a) pristine and (b) Ag doped ZnO nanorods on FTO coated glass substrate. Typical TEM micrographs of ZnO and Ag doped ZnO nanorods are shown at the insets. (c) High resolution TEM image of ZnO nanorod. (d) SAED pattern of ZnO nanorod.

(a) Full range XPS spectra of ZnO nanorods and Ag doped ZnO nanorods. Ag 3d electronic peaks are shown at the inset. (b) Zn 2p electronic states of ZnO and Ag-ZnO NRs. O1s electronic peaks of (c) ZnO NRs and (d) Ag-ZnO NRs.

(a) X-ray diffraction patterns of undoped ZnO, and Ag-doped ZnO nanorods grown at 4.5mM AgNO3 concentration. High resolution X-ray diffraction peaks of (b) (002) and (c) (101) planes for both samples.

Optical absorption spectra of ZnO NRs and Ag-ZnO NRs. Tauc’s plots ((αhν)2 vs hν) are shown at the inset. Where α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Plank’s constant and ν is the frequency of light.

(a) Current-voltage characteristics of ZnO NRs and Ag-ZnO NRs measured by scanning tunneling microscopy.
d I
d V
vs. V plots are shown at the inset. (b) The Mott-Schottky (MS) plots of ZnO NR and Ag-ZnO NR photoanodes measured at 7kHz. Ag-doped ZnO were prepared with AgNO3 concentration 4.5mM.

(a) AFM topography of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots deposited on p-Si substrate. Height distribution plot of S,N-GQDs (inset) (b) Plane view TEM micrograph of S,N-GQDs. Magnified TEM image of a typical S,N-GQDs (inset). (c) TEM image of S,N-GQD decorated Ag-ZnO NRs after detachment from FTO surface. (d) Magnified view of a typical S,N-GQD decorated Ag-ZnO NR. The attachments of S,N-GQDs are indicated by circles.

(a) Linear sweep voltammograms of S1–S4 samples recorded under white light illumination (170mWcm−2). (b) Photoconversion efficiency vs. applied bias of S1–S4 photoanodes. (c) Transient photoresponse of pristine S1–S4 under white light illumination (170mWcm−2) and without external bias.

(a) Nyquist plots (Re Z vs Im Z) of S1–S4 photoanodes under white light illumination (170mWcm−2). (b) The schematic representation of charge transport mechanism and equivalent circuit diagram of the photoanode. In equivalent circuit diagram, RS is the series resistance, CPES and RCTs are the capacitance phase element and charge transfer resistance for the depletion layer of the semiconductor, respectively. CPEd and RCTd are the capacitance phase element and charge transfer resistance at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface.
IPCE (%) vs. wavelength plots of S1–S4 photoanodes. The similar plots of all photoanodes in the wavelength range 400–700nm (inset).

Current density vs. voltage characteristics of S,N-GQD decorated Ag-ZnO NRs photoanode based photovoltaic device under illumination of light of intensity 170mWcm−2. The schematic representation of the iodine electrolyte based photovoltaic device is presented at inset.
Fitted parameters from Nyquist plots of samples S1–S4 photoanodes under dark and white light (170mWcm−2) illumination.
Photoconversion efficiency (ƞ %), short circuit current (Jsc ), IPCE (%) and RCTd (Light) of samples S1–S4.

head.ce:title: Role of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as a green photosensitizer with Ag-doped ZnO nanorods for improved electrochemical solar energy conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala, 799046, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Agartala, 799046, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties of Ag doped ZnO nanorods (Ag-ZnO NRs) have been studied in details. Ag-ZnO NRs photoanodes demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency (i.e. photon to hydrogen), photoresponse properties, incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE), compared to pristine ZnO nanorods. S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal process and utilized as a green photosensitizer for further improvement of photoelectrochemical activities. S,N-GQD sensitized nanorods showed photoconversion efficiency (η) ∼1.1%, and short circuit photocurrent (JSC ) ∼0.96mA/cm2, which are 3.4 times and 2.7 times higher than ZnO NRs. Sensitization with S,N-GQDs also improved the IPCE value and attained maximum 62.8% at 350nm, which is 2.2 times higher compared to ZnO NRs.

ce:section.ce:para: ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs) have been extensively studied as an excellent photocatalyst and photoanode for solar energy conversion, due to its large band gap (∼3.2–3.4eV), higher excitonic binding energy (60meV), high electron mobility, low synthesis cost and over all nontoxic environment friendly nature [1]. Because of wide band gap nature, ZnO can only absorb photons in the ultraviolet (UV) range (5% in the solar spectrum). Therefore, in order to obtain superior photoelectrochemical (PEC) activities, two major strategies have been followed by the several researchers, (i) doping of impurity atoms and (ii) sensitization with quantum dots (QDs). Considerable research works have been carried out for the improvement of PEC properties by doping N, Cl, Al, and C atoms in ZnO nanorods [2–5]. Wang et al. [5] synthesized N modified ZnO nanorods by ion implantation method for PEC water splitting under visible light illumination (λ>420nm).N doped ZnO nanorods showed enhanced photocurrent density of −160μA/cm2 at 1.1V vs saturated calomel electrode (SCE), which was 2 orders higher than pristine ZnO NRs. Including non-metal doping like N, Cl, C, metal doping such as Co, Al, Mo in ZnO nanorod photoanodes have been studied and exhibited improved photoelectrochemical properties [6–8]. Recently, Lin et al. [8] reported the growth and characteristics of Mo doped ZnO nanorod (Zn1-xMoxO) to improve the performance of PEC water splitting under visible light illumination. Mo doped ZnO nanorods showed pronounced photocurrent of value 0.9mA/cm2 at +1V vs. Ag/AgCl, which was 2.3 times higher than ZnO NRs. The maximum photoconversion efficiency (i.e. photon to hydrogen) was found to be 0.47%. Electrical and optical properties of ZnO NRs have been investigated by several researchers by doping novel metals like Ag [9]. Ag doped ZnO NRs exhibits strong visible photoabsorption due to the plasmonic effects and attractive for PEC application. Although, Ag doped ZnO nanorods have been utilized in different area of research such as photocatalytic study [10], solar cells [11], light emitting diodes [12], nanogenerators [13], and gas sensors [14], however, little effort has been made for practical realization in photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion. In this report, we have focused on the growth and characteristics of Ag doped ZnO nanorods for photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion.

Although, doping can improve the photoabsorption properties of ZnO, sensitization is also essential to boost the photocurrent, photoconversion efficiency (η) and incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE). Attachment of narrow band gap semiconductor QDs such as CdS [15,16], CdSe [16], CdTe [17] and PbS [18] improve the photoelectrochemical behaviours of ZnO due to strong visible photoabsorption. However, the major bottleneck of these QDs is the stability issue in aqueous solution under white light illumination [19]. More importantly, these QDs are highly toxic, not environmental friendly and may cause several health related issues. On the other hand, sensitization of ZnO NRs using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been studied to improve the PEC behaviours. GQDs have several advantages over semiconductor QDs including chemical stability, strong optical absorption in broad spectral range, low cost, nontoxic nature and large scale production capability [20–25]. Recently, the superior PEC properties of GQD sensitized ZnO NR based photoanodes have been reported by several researchers [26–28]. We have also observed superior photoconversion efficiencies with GQDs and N doped GQDs sensitized ZnO NR (η∼0.7–0.8%) compared to pristine ZnO NR photoanodes. The maximum IPCE was found to be 36.9% and 60.6%, for GQD/ZnO NRs and N-GQD/ZnO NRs, respectively, which has been enhanced from ZnO NRs (IPCE ∼26.4%) [27,28].

In this study, we reported the growth and characteristics of Ag doped ZnO NRs (Ag-ZnO NRs). PEC properties of Ag-ZnO NRs have been studied in details. S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were synthesized using low temperature hydrothermal method and utilized as a green photosensitizer with Ag-ZnO NRs to further improve the photocurrent, photoconversion efficiency and IPCE of the photoanodes.

Ag-ZnO NRs were synthesized on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates by two steps chemical process similar to the synthesis of ZnO nanorods [10]. The details experimental process has been reported elsewhere [29]. For the growth of nanorods, ZnO nanoparticle coated FTO substrates were immersed in euqimolar solutions of zinc nitrate and hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) solutions of 2, 4.5, 9mM concentration were also added to the previous solution for doping and the growth temperature was maintained at 95°C for 4h. The as grown Ag-ZnO NRs were annealed at 400°C in air for 2h for the improvement of crystalline behaviour.
•
Synthesis and attachment of S, N co-doped GQDs:

S, N-GQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal process [30]. At first, 1mM of citric acid and 3mM of thiourea were dissolved in de-ionized (DI) water. After stirring vigorously for several minutes, the solution was poured in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal reaction. The autoclave was gradually heated up to a constant temperature at 180°C for 8h and cooled down to room temperature. The as grown S,N-GQDs solution was kept for 12h for further use. Afterwards, Ag-ZnO NR samples were immersed in S,N-GQDs solutions for 2h for attachment of S,N-GQDs. The samples were washed thoroughly DI water and heated at 120°C for 3h in air environment.
•
Characterizations:

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements of Ag-ZnO NRs were carried out using a ZEISS SUPRA 40 system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study of S,N GQDs and Ag-ZnO NRs were performed using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were measured in the wavelength range 200–1000nm with a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) study of Ag doped samples were carried out using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer with CuKα (λ=0.15418nm) radiation. The chemical bonding of S,N-GQDs and Ag-ZnO NRs were investigated by a X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (PHI 5000 Versa Probe II).

Electrochemical measurements were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, USA) with three electrode configuration. In typical measurement, Ag-ZnO NRs grown on FTO coated glass substrates were utilized as the working electrodes (photoanodes). A platinum wire and Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. 0.1M aqueous solution of Na2SO4 was taken as the liquid electrolyte in the experiment. The photocurrent measurements were carried out using a broad band Xe light source (Sciencetech, Canada) and a monochromator (Sciencetech, Canada). The solar cell performance was monitored using iodine based liquid electrolyte (0.1M of I2 and KI dissolved in acetonitrile solution), which was injected in between the photoanodes and platinum coated FTO substrates. The current-voltage (J-V) measurements were performed using a source meter (B2912A, Agilent, USA) under illumination of broad band light source.

Fig. 1 (a) and (b) show the top view SEM micrographs of pristine ZnO NRs and Ag doped ZnO NRs grown on FTO coated glass substrates, respectively. Inset of Fig. 1(a) and (b) represent the typical TEM images of ZnO NRs and Ag-ZnO NRs, respectively. The average diameter of the nanorod is 130nm and density ∼1.4×109 /cm2. No microstructural changes have been observed after Ag doping in ZnO nanorods. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) study and chemical mapping confirmed the presence of the key elements like zinc, oxygen, and silver atoms in Ag-ZnO NRs (Supporting information, Fig. S1). Fig. 1(c) shows the high resolution TEM micrograph of ZnO nanorods revealing the formation of single crystalline wurtzite structure of growth direction (002) and lattice constant 0.26nm. Selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 1(d)) also confirmed the growth of single crystalline ZnO NRs along the (002) orientation.

Fig. 2 (a) represents full range XPS spectra of pristine ZnO NRs and Ag-ZnO NRs. Both spectra shows the presence of zinc and oxygen peaks which arises from ZnO nanorods. However, Ag-ZnO NRs shows an additional peak at ∼368eV, which corresponds to Ag 3d electrons [31,32]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Ag 3d electrons is plotted at inset of Fig. 2(a). The Ag 3d electronic binding energy peak has been divided into two asymmetric peaks assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2. The above peaks have been deconvoluted into two doublets Gaussian components as shown in Fig. 2(a) (inset). The peak positions at 367.5 and 373.5eV are assigned to metallic Ag or Ag2O. On the other hand, the peaks at 368.5 and 374.5 are attributed to Ag-Zn-O ternary compound [31]. Fig. 2(b) shows high resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p electrons, displaying doublet peaks corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 core level electrons. Interestingly, the peaks for Ag doped ZnO nanorods have been shifted to higher binding energies compared to pristine ZnO nanorods. The higher energy shifting of Zn 2p electrons in Ag-ZnO NRs confirmed the presence of additional Ag-Zn-O chemical bonding state [31]. Fig. 2(c) and (d) represent the high resolution XPS spectra of O 1s electronic levels for pristine ZnO and Ag-ZnO nanorods, respectively. The O 1s electronic peak of undoped ZnO NRs has been deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks, located at 530.2 and 531.6eV. On the other hand, O 1s peak of Ag-ZnO NRs has been fitted into three Gaussian peaks situated at 530.2, 531.6 and 533.1eV. The common peaks at 530.2 and 531.6eV for both samples are attributed to Zn-O-Zn (ZnOx) and hydroxyl species (ZnOx(OH)y) at the surface state of ZnO, respectively [31]. Interestingly, when the Ag dopants were introduced, the intensity ratio of ZnOx(OH)y and ZnOx peaks has been increased significantly, which was also observed by Lupan et al. [31] However, the additional binding energy peak at 533.1eV for Ag-ZnO NRs indicates the formation of Ag-Zn-O ternary compound [31].

Fig. 3 (a) shows the XRD patterns of ZnO and Ag-ZnO NRs grown on FTO coated glass substrates. XRD study confirmed that, Ag-ZnO NRs (grown at 2, 5, 10mM AgNO3 concentration) are highly crystalline. The well defined peaks were indexed to the standard diffraction pattern of hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO (Supporting information, Fig. S2). Fig. 3(b) and (c) represents the high resolution scan of (002) and (101) peaks for both samples. In has been observed that, the diffraction peaks of Ag-ZnO NRs has been shifted towards lower 2theta value, which is caused by the replacement of Zn+ (radius ∼0.74Å) ion by larger atomic radii Ag+ ion (radius ∼1.15Å) [10,31]. Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO and Ag doped ZnO nanorods. The absorption spectrum of ZnO NRs revealed characteristic absorption edge near 385nm, which is related to the band edge transitions of ZnO [32]. On the other hand, Ag-ZnO NRs showed board absorbance at visible region (420–770nm) compared to ZnO NRs. To estimate the absorption edge, Tauc’s plots ((αhν)2 vs hν)] for both samples are shown at the inset of Fig. 4. Interestingly, the absorption edge of Ag- doped ZnO has been shifted to lower energy (∼2.8eV) compared to pristine ZnO, which is due to the Ag doping effect [10,33].

The current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of doped and undoped nanorods were recorded by scanning tunnelling microscope using a tungsten tip under ambient condition (Multimode 8, Bruker). Fig. 5 (a) shows STM I–V characteristics of ZnO and Ag-ZnO NRs. The tunnelling currents were measured under the applied bias −1V to +1V at 10 various locations of the sample. I–V characteristics of both samples were plotted by averaging all results. Inset of Fig. 5(a) shows the tunnelling current conductance (dI/dV) vs bias voltage (V) plots. Interestingly, at all applied bias, tunnelling current for Ag-ZnO NRs is higher compared to ZnO nanorods, which is attributed to the Ag doping effect.

The flat band voltages and donor concentrations have been estimated from Mott-Schottky (MS) plots [29,34]. In flat band condition, the capacitance of a surface can be written as [29,34].
(1)
1
C 2
=
2
e ε
ε 0
N d
A s
2
[
(
V −
V
F B
)
−
k T
e
]

The symbols e,ε 0 , є, Nd, k, As, T, VFB, V are electronic charge, permittivity in free space, dielectric constant of ZnO, donor concentration, Boltzman constant, sample area, absolute temperature, flat band potential and applied bias, respectively. The Mott-Schottky (MS) plots (
1
C 2
vs V) of ZnO NRs and Ag-ZnO NRs are presented in Fig. 5(b). Flat band voltages (VFB) have been extracted from the x-axis intercepts of MS plot. For ZnO nanorods, VFB is −0.35V vs Ag/AgCl, which is similar to the reported results [34,35]. Interestingly, VFB for Ag-ZnO NRs has been shifted to right side of voltage axis and equals to −0.29V vs. Ag/AgCl, which is attributed to the silver doping effect [33]. However, both samples showed positive slope in linear region of MS plots, indicating the presence of donor type impurities [34,35]. The donor concentrations (Nd) was determined from the slope of the linear region of MS plot using the following equation [34,35].
(2)
N d
= −
(
2
e ε
ε 0
)
(
d
(
1
C 2
)
d V
)
− 1

The donor concentration of ZnO NRs is found to be ∼1.5×1019 cm−3, which is slightly higher than the reported results [35]. On the other hand, Nd value for Ag-ZnO NRs is 1.74×1020 cm−3. The improvement of carrier density is due to the doping effect of silver atoms in ZnO nanorods, which corroborates our STM results.

To demonstrate the role of grapheme quantum dots as a green photosensitizer with ZnO nanorods for photoelectrochemical applications, we have synthesized S,N-GQDs and attached with Ag-ZnO NRs. Fig. 6 (a) represents the AFM surface topography of as grown S, N-GQDs dispersed on p-Si substrates. The height distribution plot is presented at the inset of Fig. 6(a). The thickness of S,N-GQDs are in the range 0.5–5nm and more than 81% S,N-GQDs are consisted of 1–3 monolayer of graphene. S and N atoms doping in GQDs has been confirmed by XPS study (Supporting information, Fig. S3). Fig. 6(b) shows the plane view TEM image of S,N-GQDs of diameter 3–6nm. The magnified TEM image (inset of Fig. 6b) confirmed that, S,N-GQDs are well crystalline nature with lattice spacing 0.34nm, which indicates the presence of (002) facet of graphitic carbon [28]. The quantum dots were attached with Ag-ZnO NRs by dipping in aqueous solution of S,N-GQDs for 2h. Fig. 6(c) shows the bright field TEM micrograph of S,N-GQDs decorated Ag-ZnO NRs. The average length and diameter of the nanorods was estimated from TEM micrographs and found to be 1.1μm and 120nm, respectively. The magnified TEM image of a typical S, N-GQD decorated ZnO nanorod is presented in Fig. 6(d). The micrograph revealed several small circular spots (indicated by circles) corresponding to the attachment of S, N-GQDs. The attachment of S,N-GQDs was further confirmed by XPS and Raman study and discussed in Supporting information (Fig. S4, S5).

To study the photoelectrochemical properties, the following samples have been studied. The samples are (i) ZnO NRs/FTO (Sample S1), (ii) Ag-ZnO NRs/FTO (Sample S2), (iii) S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs/FTO (Sample S3) and (iv) S,N-GQD/Ag-ZnO NRs/FTO (Sample S4). Fig. 7 (a) represents the linear sweep voltammograms (J-V) of samples S1–S4 under illumination of broadband light of intensity 170mW/cm2. At dark condition, the current density (J) values for all samples are very small (∼10–80μAcm−2 at 1V vs Ag/AgCl). But under illumination, the current density has been increased for all samples. As for example, the photocurrent density for S1–S4 photoanodes are 1.22, 1.72, 2.59 and 2.60mA/cm2 at 1V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. It is worth mentioning here that, the photoconductive nature of Ag doped ZnO NRs depend on the Ag doping effect. In our present study, maximum photocurrent was obtained for Ag-ZnO NRs grown at 4.5mM AgNO3 concentration (Supporting information, Fig. S6). In all photoelectrochemical measurements, we have studied the above Ag-ZnO NRs samples for comparison. Fig. 7(b) shows the variation of photoconversion efficiency (η) as a function of external bias vs. Ag/AgCl for all photoanodes. The photoconversion efficiencies were calculated from the following relation [36].
(3)
η
=
J
(
1.23 −
V
applied
)
P in
where J is current density, Vapplied is the applied potential vs Ag/AgCl, and Pin is the power of the incident light. The maximum value for S1–S4 samples are 0.32% (at 0.55V vs. Ag/AgCl), 0.56% (at 0.40V vs. Ag/AgCl), 0.85% (at 0.45V vs. Ag/AgCl) and 1.1% (at 0.29V vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. Fig. 7(c) shows the transient photocurrents (J-t curves) of S1–S4 photoanodes under white light illumination of intensity 170mW/cm2. The transient photocurrent response measurements were carried out at 0V external bias condition. Under illumination, the short circuit photocurrent densities (JSC ) for S1–S4 photoanodes are 0.35mA/cm2, 0.68mA/cm2, 0.77mA/cm2 and 0.97mA/cm2, respectively. Interestingly, the JSC for Ag doped ZnO nanorods enhances 1.9 times larger than pristine ZnO nanorods. More importantly, for sample S4, JSC increases 2.7 times higher than S1. The maximum photocurrent in S,N-GQD modified Ag-ZnO nanorods is attributed to strong photoabsorption in visible region, cascade band structure, better charge separation and charge carrier transfer at the nanorods/S,N-GQD interfaces.

The interface properties of sample S4/electrolyte junctions have been investigated by using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study in the frequency interval 1Hz–2MHz under dark and light. Fig. 8 (a) represents the plot of real (Re Z) and imaginary part (Im Z) of complex impedance (Z) of all photoanodes upon illumination. The equivalent circuit diagram and charge transport mechanism at nanorods/electrolyte interface is shown at Fig. 8(b). The extracted values of the fitted parameters CPES, RCTs, CPEd and RCTd for all photoanodes are presented in Table 1 . The symbols CPES, RCTs are capacitance phase element and charge transfer resistance for the depletion layer of the semiconductor. And CPEd, RCTd are the capacitance phase element and charge transfer resistance at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface, respectively. Under dark condition, the charge transfer resistance RCTd for S1 is higher than S2 (Supporting information Fig. S7, Table-I). More importantly, the above resistances have been reduced further after attachment of S, N-GQDs. Under illumination of light, the RCTd values decreases sharply for all samples. As for example, at dark RCTd(dark) values for S1–S4 are 38.1, 35.1, 35.7 and 34.6KΩcm−2, respectively (Table 1). Under illumination, RCTd(light) values for the above samples are 297, 239, 158 and 123Ωcm−2, respectively. The rapid drop off of RCTd in S, N-GQD sensitized nanorods is due to faster charge transfer and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs at nanorod/electrolyte interface [37].

To study the role of S, N-GQDs for enhancing the photocurrent and photoconversion efficiency of Ag-ZnO nanorods, IPCE measurements were performed at 0V vs Ag/AgCl. The IPCE was estimated using the following relation [38]
(4)
I P C E
( % )
=
1240
J λ
λ
P λ
× 100
where J λ is photocurrent density, λ is wavelength of light and Pλ is the monochromatic light intensity. Fig. 9 shows the IPCE (%) spectra of all photoanodes. The maximum IPCE (%) for S1–S4 samples are found to be 28.6%, 36.5%, 59.4% and 62.8% at 350nm wavelength. Inset of Fig. 9 shows IPCE plot for all samples in the wavelength range 400–700nm. Interestingly, S,N-GQD sensitized nanorods showed higher IPCE value not only at UV region but also at visible range, which is attributed to strong visible photoabsorption. The IPCE study also supports our UV–vis absorption results (Supporting information, Fig. S8). In this present study, we have improved the photoelectrochemical properties such as short circuit current (JSC ), photoconversion efficiency and IPCE values of ZnO nanorods by doping with Ag atoms. The above parameters have been further enhanced by sensitising the nanorods with S,N-GQDs. All results have been summarized in Table 2 . It is obvious that, the superior photoelectrochemical behaviors of S, N-GQD/Ag-ZnO NRs is attributed to the strong photoabsorption, less interfacial resistance, efficient hole separation at the nanorod/electrolyte interface due to the band cascade formation.

We have also studied the photovoltaic performance of S,N-GQD sensitized Ag-ZnO NR photoanodes. Fig. 10 shows the J-V characteristics of the photoanode under illumination of light of intensity 170mWcm−2. The typical device structure is shown at the inset of Fig. 10. Purely iodine electrolyte based electrochemical device exhibits short circuit current (J SC)∼2.37mAcm−2, open circuit voltage (VOC)∼ 0.37V and power conversion efficiency (ξP) ∼0.14%, which is higher than the others reported results [39–42].

In summary, we have grown Ag doped ZnO nanorods on FTO coated glass substrates by hydrothermal process. The doping of Ag atoms in ZnO nanorods was confirmed by EDX, XPS and XRD study. Ag doped nanorods are n-type nature and the carrier concentrations (Nd ∼2.1×1020 cm−3) was found to be higher compared to pristine ZnO NRs (Nd ∼1.5×1019 cm−3). Ag-ZnO NRs demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency, photoresponse properties, incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency, compared to undoped nanorods. Interestingly, the above parameters have been increased further after attachment of S,N-GQD, which act as a green photosensitizer. The photovoltaic performance of S,N-GQD decorated Ag-ZnO NR photoanodes was demonstrated using iodine electrolyte and Pt films counter electrode. This electrochemical cell showed 3.5 times higher power conversion efficiency compared to ZnO NRs based photoanodes, which is superior to others reported results. Our present study established the dominant role of S,NGQD decorated Ag-ZnO NRs as a green, ecofriendly, low-cost photoanode for solar energy conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was partially funded by CSIR Extramural Research Grant, Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We also acknowledge the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for SEM, UV-VIS-NIR spectroscopy and XRD characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at 10.1016/j.materresbull.2017.05.004.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc oxide nanostructures: growth properties and applications
